steps.action: The Mazurka, alongside the polka dance, became p with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: The Mazurka, alongside the polka dance, became p with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: The mazurka (in Polish mazur, the same word as t
steps.text: The mazurka (in Polish mazur, the same word as the mazur) and mazurek (rural dance based on the mazur) are often confused in Western literature as the same musical form.

steps.action: In relation to dance, all these words (mazur, ma
steps.text: In relation to dance, all these words (mazur, mazurek, mazurka) mean "a Mazovian dance".

steps.action: Mazurek is also a rural dance identified by some
steps.text: Mazurek is also a rural dance identified by some as the oberek.

steps.action: It is said oberek is a danced variation of the s
steps.text: It is said oberek is a danced variation of the sung mazurek, the latter also having more prominent accents on second and third beats and less fluent of a rhythmical flow, which is so characteristical of oberek.

steps.action: The stylistic and musical characteristics of his
steps.text: The stylistic and musical characteristics of his mazurkas differ from the traditional variety because Chopin in effect created a more complex type of mazurka, using classical techniques, including counterpoint and fugue.

steps.action: By including more chromaticism and harmony in th
steps.text: By including more chromaticism and harmony in the mazurkas, he made them more technically interesting than the traditional dances.

steps.action: Chopin also tried to compose his mazurkas in suc
steps.text: Chopin also tried to compose his mazurkas in such a way that they could not be used for dancing, so as to distance them from the original form.

steps.action: His mazurkas, like the traditional dances, conta
steps.text: His mazurkas, like the traditional dances, contain a great deal of repetition: repetition of certain measures or groups of measures; of entire sections; and of an initial theme.

steps.action: The rhythm of his mazurkas also remains very sim
steps.text: The rhythm of his mazurkas also remains very similar to that of earlier mazurkas.

steps.action: This use of rhythm suggests that Chopin tried to
steps.text: This use of rhythm suggests that Chopin tried to create a genre that had ties to the original form, but was still something new and different.

steps.action: The mazurka began as a dance for either four or
steps.text: The mazurka began as a dance for either four or eight couples.

steps.action: Eventually, Michel Fokine created a female solo
steps.text: Eventually, Michel Fokine created a female solo mazurka dance dominated by flying grandes jetés, alternating second and third arabesque positions, and split-leg climactic postures.

steps.action: It also takes a variation of the mazurka dance f
steps.text: It also takes a variation of the mazurka dance form and is found mostly in the north of the archipelago, mainly in São Nicolau, Santo Antão.

steps.action: The mazurka appears frequently in French traditi
steps.text: The mazurka appears frequently in French traditional folk music.

steps.action: In the French Antilles, the mazurka has become a
steps.text: In the French Antilles, the mazurka has become an important style of dance and music.

steps.action: This "sexy" style of mazurka has also been impor
steps.text: This "sexy" style of mazurka has also been imported to “balfolk" dancing in Belgium and the Netherlands, hence the name "Belgian Mazurka" or "Flemish Mazurka".

steps.action: As a couple's dance, it is no longer popular.
steps.text: As a couple's dance, it is no longer popular.

steps.action: The Polish dance entered Ireland in the 1840s, b
steps.text: The Polish dance entered Ireland in the 1840s, but is not widely played outside of Donegal.

steps.action: Unlike the Polish mazurek, which may have an acc
steps.text: Unlike the Polish mazurek, which may have an accent on the second or third beat of a bar, the Irish mazurka (masúrca in the Irish language) is consistently accented on the second beat, giving it a unique feel.

steps.action: Musician Caoimhín Mac Aoidh has written a book o
steps.text: Musician Caoimhín Mac Aoidh has written a book on the subject, From Mazovia to Meenbanad: The Donegal Mazurkas, in which the history of the musical and dance form is related.

steps.action: Typical of Italian mazurkas are groups of triple
steps.text: Typical of Italian mazurkas are groups of triplets, strong dotted rhythms, and phrase endings of two accented quarter notes and a rest, unlike a waltz.

steps.action: The mazurka was a common dance at the balls of t
steps.text: The mazurka was a common dance at the balls of the Russian Empire and it is depicted in many Russian novels and films.

steps.action: In addition to its mention in Leo Tolstoy's Anna
steps.text: In addition to its mention in Leo Tolstoy's Anna Karenina as well as in a protracted episode in War and Peace, the dance is prominently featured in Ivan Turgenev's novel Fathers and Sons.
